overview: Long Neck Point is a narrow peninsula extending into Long Island Sound at Darien's southernmost reach, culminating in a gated enclave of fewer than a dozen waterfront estates. The Darien Boat Club, at the peninsula's base, anchors an active maritime culture with more than 850 member families. Properties here offer direct Long Island Sound frontage, private beaches, and unobstructed views stretching west toward the Manhattan skyline.

children.text: Long Neck Point Road runs south from the Post Road and narrows as it follows the peninsula's spine toward the Sound. The road can span as few as two building lots in width at its tightest points, making this one of the most geographically intimate waterfront addresses on the Connecticut Gold Coast. The gated section at the tip — a private enclave accessible only to residents — contains some of the rarest estate parcels on the Darien shoreline. Properties at that reach rarely surface on the open market, and when they do, they draw buyers accustomed to comparable addresses in Southampton or Palm Beach.

The architecture along the peninsula is deliberately varied. Grand shingle-style cottages from the early twentieth century sit alongside French country chateaus and contemporary estates rebuilt in the past two decades. Lot sizes expand considerably toward the southern terminus, where mature plantings frame sloping lawns that descend to private beaches and docks. The shoreline configuration — water on multiple sides for the southernmost parcels — gives several properties exposure to both easterly sunrises and the westerly sunsets with Manhattan in the frame.

The Darien Boat Club has operated on Long Neck Point since the community's founding era and now serves more than 850 member families. The Club provides slips, moorings, and sailing programs — central to a neighborhood where proximity to the water is the primary organizing principle of residential life. A public beach at the peninsula's base gives the broader community a connection to the shoreline that the private enclave at the tip intensifies rather than replaces.

Children in the neighborhood attend Ox Ridge Elementary School before progressing to Middlesex Middle School and Darien High School, which ranked second in Connecticut on the U.S. News & World Report 2024 list. The entire district carries a five-star state rating. The Post Road, with its grocery options, pharmacies, and restaurants, is roughly five minutes north along Long Neck Point Road — close enough to be convenient, far enough to be irrelevant to daily peninsula life.

highlights: Gated waterfront enclave at peninsula's tip with private beaches, docks, and Long Island Sound frontage
Darien Boat Club on-site — slips, moorings, and sailing programs for 850+ member families
Westerly views to the Manhattan skyline from the peninsula's southernmost estates
Listed prices range from $9 million to $29.5 million for waterfront estate parcels
Ox Ridge Elementary feeds into top-ranked Darien High (#2 in CT) and Middlesex Middle School
housingCharacteristics:
Housing stock on Long Neck Point reflects over a century of high-end coastal construction. Shingle-style cottages, Colonial Revival homes, and French country chateaus appear alongside fully rebuilt contemporary estates, the oldest dating to the early 1900s. Properties become progressively larger and more private as the road narrows toward the tip — interior lots near the peninsula's base might sit on half an acre, while the gated southern estates occupy several acres of land with substantial waterfront footage. Several properties include private pools set within curated grounds that transition to natural shoreline.

Pricing on Long Neck Point is among the highest in Darien and on the Connecticut coast. Waterfront listings have ranged from approximately $9 million to $29.5 million in recent years, with trophy parcels at the gated southern tip commanding the upper end of that range. Properties without direct waterfront but within the peninsula still command significant premiums over Darien's broader market median.
marketNotes:
Long Neck Point operates as one of Fairfield County's thinnest markets. Turnover in the gated enclave at the tip is measured in years between transactions, not months. When a property becomes available in that section, the buyer pool is self-selecting — typically individuals who have studied the address and are prepared to move decisively. Non-waterfront properties along the peninsula mid-section trade with somewhat more frequency but still at values well above Darien's town-wide median of roughly $2.4 million.

Buyers should expect limited negotiating leverage on well-presented properties, as demand routinely exceeds supply. The lack of comparable sales data — driven by infrequent transactions — means pricing analysis often requires examining sales from two or three years prior. Sellers, in turn, hold significant positional advantage whenever a property is correctly presented and priced to its unique attributes.
locationAccess:
Long Neck Point Road begins at the Post Road (Route 1) and runs south approximately one mile to the peninsula's tip. The drive to Darien's Metro-North station — with express service reaching Grand Central Terminal in approximately 57 minutes — takes seven to ten minutes without traffic. Interstate 95 is accessible at Exit 11 within five minutes heading north on the Post Road.

The Noroton Heights Metro-North station, with the slightly faster express run of about 54 minutes to Grand Central, is also reachable within ten minutes. Both Darien Commons and the core Post Road retail corridor — Palmer's Market, CVS, and a range of restaurants — are within a short drive north. The peninsula's geography means through traffic is structurally impossible, which preserves the residential character throughout the year.
seoTitle: Long Neck Point Darien CT | Waterfront Estates
seoDescription: Long Neck Point is Darien's most exclusive waterfront peninsula — gated estates, private beaches, and Manhattan views. Contact us to see available listings.

faqs.question: Is Long Neck Point in Darien a gated community?
faqs.answer: The southernmost section of Long Neck Point is a private gated enclave containing fewer than a dozen waterfront estates. The remainder of the peninsula is residential but not gated, accessible via Long Neck Point Road from the Post Road. The gated enclave at the tip represents some of the rarest and most expensive residential parcels on the Connecticut shoreline, with properties trading infrequently and at prices typically ranging from $9 million to well above $20 million.

faqs.question: What are home prices on Long Neck Point in Darien, CT?
faqs.answer: Waterfront properties on Long Neck Point have listed between approximately $9 million and $29.5 million in recent years, depending on lot size, water frontage, and position within the peninsula. Properties in the gated southern enclave command the upper end of the range. Non-waterfront parcels along the mid-peninsula section trade at significant premiums over Darien's town-wide median but well below the trophy estate tier.

faqs.question: What school does Long Neck Point feed into?
faqs.answer: Children in the Long Neck Point area attend Ox Ridge Elementary School for grades K through 5, then advance to Middlesex Middle School, and ultimately Darien High School, which ranked second in Connecticut in the 2024 U.S. News & World Report high school rankings. The Darien School District holds a five-star state rating and spends approximately $26,000 per student annually.

faqs.question: What is the commute from Long Neck Point to New York City?
faqs.answer: The nearest Metro-North station is Darien station on the New Haven Line, a seven-to-ten-minute drive from Long Neck Point. Express service from Darien station reaches Grand Central Terminal in approximately 57 minutes. The Noroton Heights station, also reachable in about ten minutes, offers a slightly shorter express run of around 54 minutes. Interstate 95 via Exit 11 provides a highway alternative for those driving into New York or Westchester.
